Question.content: Arrange butane, methylpropane and cyclobutene in order of increasing boiling point

Explanation.content:
Boiling Point Trends:

  1. Methylpropane (isobutane): -11.7°C
    • Branched → weak London dispersion forces
  2. Butane: -0.5°C
    • Linear → stronger intermolecular forces
  3. Cyclobutene: 2-4°C
    • Ring strain + slight polarity from C=C

Key factor: Surface area of contact between molecules

Question.content: Arrange pentane, 2-methylbutane and 2,2-dimethylpropane in an increasing order of boiling point

Explanation.content:
Boiling Point Trends (C5H12 isomers):

CompoundBP (°C)Branching
Pentane36.1Linear
2-methylbutane27.81 branch
2,2-dimethylpropane9.52 branches

More branching → weaker London forces → lower BP

Question.content: Optical isomers that are mirror images of each other are called?

Explanation.content:
Enantiomers are:

  • Non-superimposable mirror images
  • Have identical physical properties except for optical rotation
  • Rotate plane-polarized light equally but in opposite directions

Example: L- and D-alanine

🔄 Mirror relationship

name: Question 11 @ 2022/2023

Question.content: Underline the compound(s) among the following that can exhibit geometrical isomerism: 1-butene, 2-butyne, 2-pentene, 2-methylpentane

Explanation.content:
Geometrical isomerism (cis/trans) requires:

  • A double bond (C=C) ✓ Present in 1-butene and 2-pentene
  • Different groups on each double bond carbon ✓ Only 2-pentene meets this

2-pentene examples:

cis: CH3-CH=CH-CH2-CH3 (same side)
trans: CH3...CH3 (opposite sides)

Question.content: Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of boiling point: butane, 2-methyl propane, cyclobutane

Explanation.content:
Boiling point factors:

Compound BP (°C) Reason
2-methyl propane -11.7 Branched → weak London forces
butane -0.5 Linear → stronger London forces
cyclobutane 12.5 Ring strain → more rigid structure

Question.content: Draw the structure of the functional group isomer of ethanol

Explanation.content:
Functional group isomers have:

  • Same molecular formula (C2H6O)
  • Different functional groups

Ethanol: CH3-CH2-OH (alcohol)

Isomer: CH3-O-CH3 (ether)

Question.content: Arrange n-hexane, cyclohexane and 3-methyl pentane in an order of decreasing boiling point

Explanation.content:
• Cyclohexane has the highest boiling point due to its rigid cyclic structure allowing closer molecular packing
  • n-Hexane (straight chain) has stronger London dispersion forces than branched 3-methylpentane
  • 3-Methylpentane has the lowest boiling point because branching reduces surface area and intermolecular forces

Question.content: Account for the difference in boiling points between methoxymethane and ethanol, although both have the same molecular formula

Answer.content: Ethanol can form hydrogen bonding whereas methoxymethane cannot.

Explanation.content:
• Ethanol (CH3CH2OH): Forms strong intermolecular hydrogen bonds (O-H⋯O) requiring more energy to break
  • Methoxymethane (CH3OCH3): Only has weaker dipole-dipole interactions (no H-bonding)
  • Hydrogen bonding increases ethanol's boiling point by ≈20°C compared to methoxymethane

Question.content: What type of isomerism exists in 2-chloropentane and 3-chloropentane

Explanation.content:
• Positional isomers have the same molecular formula but differ in the position of functional groups
  • Both compounds are C5H11Cl with chlorine at different carbon positions (C-2 vs C-3)
  • They have different physical properties and slightly different chemical reactivity

Question.content: Draw the structure and give the IUPAC name of an alkane with the molecular formula \(C_5H_{12}\) that has only primary hydrogens

Explanation.content:
• Only 2,2-dimethylpropane (neopentane) satisfies this condition
  • Central quaternary carbon with four methyl groups
  • All 12 hydrogens are equivalent and primary
  • No secondary or tertiary hydrogens present
